text: For the wifi it needs a big yagi antenna on top.

author: @reypolice5231
text:
@ Robert Murray Smith

Looks like you had a lot of fun there. Great job it looks awesome.

My only concern is that the conductive paint will pick up a static charge and if you touch the painted service it might give you a shock.

So perhaps you might want to ground that surface to avoid getting a nasty shock?

thanks for the fun video I had a good laugh seeing it get put together.

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text:
If you were to use everything known today to make the most efficient driving system using heat & electricity, and whatever else may have to work together (while no inspiration from how any functional existing motors work), how would you go about it? What would it look like, what would you make? How would you go about making the most efficient thing possible? 
Like... using all the science in one device..?

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text: <poke> Do more science

author: @Edsbar
text: I love your videos with the have ago enthusiasm but I can't help being interested in your background. You mention odd bits like your inks but would you do a video about what you do in that unit other than playing with cool stuff. I do apologize if you think I'm being nosey but I'd say interested is a better way to put it.

author: @johnrussell4125
text: WOW, I never new the Victorians could do that!

replies.author: @SlyerFox666
replies.text: They couldn't,  It's Futuristic, yet retro at the same time. The genre blends the aesthetic and technology of the 19th century with elements of science fiction. The steampunk aesthetic is inspired by the fashions of Victorian Era in England (1837-1901), but also by the Belle Epoque in France (1871-1914) and the Civil War era in the United States (1861-1865).
